prompt: Build a REST API with authentication, database, and CRUD operations for a blog system

requirements: Build a REST API with authentication
Create CRUD operations for blog system
execution_steps: Design the database schema and create the necessary tables
Choose an authentication method (e.g. JWT, OAuth) and implement it in the API
Write the REST API endpoints for CRUD operations on blog posts
Implement API routing and handling for requests

requirements: Build a REST API with authentication
Create CRUD operations for blog system
constraints: Database schema and table design may impact CRUD performance
Authentication method choice can affect scalability and security
API endpoint design should consider RESTful principles
recommended_technologies: Node.js with Express.js framework for API development
PostgreSQL or MongoDB as database management systems
best_practices: Follow SOLID principles for code organization and maintainability
Implement proper error handling and logging mechanisms
Use a consistent coding style throughout the project
potential_challenges: Managing concurrent requests and database transactions
Securing authentication tokens and user data
Handling large amounts of blog post data for CRUD operations

api_contracts.description: Get a single blog post by ID

design_decisions: Using microservices architecture to separate concerns and improve scalability.
JWT for authentication due to its stateless nature and ease of implementation.

implementation.description: A REST API with authentication, database integration, and CRUD operations for a blog system.
completed_steps: Design the database schema and create the necessary tables
Choose an authentication method (JWT) and implement it in the API
Write the REST API endpoints for CRUD operations on blog posts
Implement API routing and handling for requests

issues.description: Missing error handling for database queries in blog CRUD functions

recommendations: Implement proper error handling for database queries in blog CRUD functions
Store JWT secret securely, e.g., using environment variables or a secrets manager
